summary: Gruber responds to Matt Yglesias's suggestion that Apple should cut Mac prices to gain market share, arguing this fundamentally misunderstands Apple's strategy. He notes that Apple already captures 45% of PC industry profits despite modest unit share. Gruber contends that price isn't the primary barrier to Mac adoption — familiarity with Windows is — and that price-sensitive customers gained through discounts wouldn't feed the Apple ecosystem. He argues the halo effect only works because Apple maintains consistent premium pricing across all product lines, and that this consistency is what converts first-time buyers into multi-product customers.
key_points: Apple captures 45% of PC market profits while holding a small fraction of unit market share, mirroring its phone strategy
Price isn't the main barrier to Mac adoption — Windows familiarity and resistance to change matter more
Customers gained through price cuts would be price-sensitive and unlikely to buy premium iPhones and iPads, undermining the halo effect
The halo effect depends on consistent pricing and positioning across all Apple products — discount one and the coherence breaks
Apple's strategy is to target only high-margin market segments and forgo low-margin volume, the opposite of conventional wisdom
money_quotes: It's not within Apple's character to pursue market share for the sake of market share.

A lot of people buy Windows PCs specifically because Windows is what they're familiar with; they don't want to even consider a Mac for the same reason many of them are rejecting Windows 8: too different.

By definition, the new customers they'd gain from slashing the price of Mac hardware would be price-sensitive customers. Why would those customers buy iPhones and iPads unless the prices of iPhones and iPads were also similarly slashed?
Macs, iPads, iPhones, and iPods are all just Apple products, and they're all priced and designed the same way: seldom the cheapest, but usually the best.
It's their consistency in that regard across all products that drives the halo effect, turning someone who never bought an Apple product in their life into someone with an iPhone, iPad, and MacBook.

The conventional wisdom is to pursue profits by maximizing market share. Apple pursues profits by specifically targeting only the high-margin segments of the overall market, and effectively forgoing market share in the low-margin segments, no matter how large those low-margin segments are.
key_insight: Apple's halo effect depends on consistent premium positioning across all products — discounting one category to chase market share would undermine the brand coherence that converts single-product buyers into ecosystem customers.
